content.description: go.mod pins go 1.26.2 vs go 1.26+toolchain; ExampleClient_Sync real network I/O; client_test.go tb.Fatalf from goroutines; Sync/PlanRequest.Validate duplicates. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.text: `buildProbeConfig` omits `Mappings` by design — `ProbeRequest` doesn't even carry a `Scope`/`Mappings` (probe is pure ref inspection scoped only by all-refs/tags/exclude). So the fetch fix is the right and complete scope; the change reuses `validationMappings` exactly like its siblings. `/simplify` finds nothing. Pushing and opening the M3 PR:

input.command:
git push -u origin fix/fetch-drops-mappings 2>&1 | tail -2 && gh pr create --base main --head fix/fetch-drops-mappings --title "Carry Scope.Mappings into the fetch config" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

`buildFetchConfig` copied `Branches`, `AllRefs`, and `ExcludeRefPrefixes` from the request scope but never set `Mappings` — unlike its `buildSyncConfig` and `buildBootstrapConfig` siblings. A mappings-scoped `Fetch` therefore silently ignored the mappings and fetched the wrong refs (or errored confusingly).

## Fix

Populate `Mappings: validationMappings(req.Scope.Mappings)`, identical to the sync/bootstrap config builders.

(`buildProbeConfig` legitimately has no `Mappings` — `ProbeRequest` carries no scope mappings; probe is pure ref inspection.)

## Tests

`TestBuildFetchConfigPreservesMappings` asserts a `FetchRequest` with a `Scope.Mappings` entry yields a syncer config carrying that mapping through.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2

content.text: Baseline confirmed. Let me read the top of pack_observer.go to understand the tee/pipe wiring:

content.content:
1	package bootstrap
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"io"
7		"sync"
8		"sync/atomic"
9	
10		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/format/packfile"
11	)
12	
13	// ErrPackUploadAborted is returned from packStreamObserver.Read when
14	// the configured aborter says the upload is projected to exceed the
15	// target body limit. Surfaces up through the HTTP transport as a
16	// generic body-read error; bootstrap's push-failed branch checks the
17	// observer's Aborted() flag to distinguish "we cut it" from a
18	// server-side 413 / 500 / network failure.
19	var ErrPackUploadAborted = errors.New("pack upload aborted early: projected to exceed target body limit")
20	
21	// packStreamObserver wraps the pack stream handed to PushPack with two
22	// instruments:
23	//
24	//  1. a byte counter (replacing the simpler packReadCounter), so the
25	//     bootstrap loop still learns how much data went up before a 413
26	//     for post-rejection sizing;
27	//  2. a streaming pack-format observer that tees the bytes through
28	//     packfile.Scanner running on a goroutine, exposing how many of
29	//     the pack header's advertised objects have actually been sent
30	//     across the wire so far.
31	//
32	// The (objects-sent, bytes-sent, total-objects) triple lets later
33	// callers project the real size before the server cuts the upload —
34	// which is the prerequisite for a mid-stream early abort. Until that
35	// abort path is added, the observer is a strict superset of the
36	// previous packReadCounter and only reports observed counters.
37	//
38	// The Scanner runs on a goroutine reading from the tee'd pipe; if it
39	// falls behind the HTTP upload, the unbuffered pipe back-pressures the
40	// upload until Scanner catches up. zlib decoding inside Scanner is the
41	// dominant cost and runs at 200–500 MB/s per core on modern hardware,
42	// which leaves substantial headroom over typical wire upload speeds.
43	// If profiling later shows the observer bottlenecking real pushes, the
44	// fix is a custom format walker that skips Scanner's per-object SHA-1
45	// hashing — but the zlib walk itself is unavoidable because pack
46	// objects don't record their compressed size.
47	type packStreamObserver struct {
48		src io.ReadCloser
49		tee io.Reader
50		pw  *io.PipeWriter
51	
52		bytes        atomic.Int64
53		objectsSent  atomic.Int64
54		totalObjects atomic.Int64
55	
56		headerOnce  sync.Once
57		headerReady chan struct{}
58		done        chan struct{}
59		scannerErr  atomic.Pointer[error]
60	
61		aborter aborterFunc
62		aborted atomic.Bool
63	}
64	
65	// aborterFunc is consulted on every Read to decide whether the upload
66	// should be cancelled mid-stream. Receives the latest counters so it
67	// can project the final pack size from the bytes-per-object ratio so
68	// far. Return true to abort the upload; the observer surfaces
69	// ErrPackUploadAborted from its next Read and refuses to give up
70	// further bytes.
71	type aborterFunc func(bytesSent, objectsSent, totalObjects int64) bool
72	
73	func newPackStreamObserver(src io.ReadCloser) *packStreamObserver {
74		pr, pw := io.Pipe()
75		obs := &packStreamObserver{
76			src:         src,
77			pw:          pw,
78			headerReady: make(chan struct{}),
79			done:        make(chan struct{}),
80		}
81		obs.tee = io.TeeReader(src, pw)
82		go obs.consume(pr)
83		return obs
84	}
85	
86	func (o *packStreamObserver) Read(p []byte) (int, error) {
87		if o.aborted.Load() {
88			return 0, ErrPackUploadAborted
89		}
90		n, err := o.tee.Read(p)
91		if n > 0 {
92			o.bytes.Add(int64(n))
93		}
94		if err == nil && o.aborter != nil {
95			if o.aborter(o.bytes.Load(), o.objectsSent.Load(), o.totalObjects.Load()) {
96				o.aborted.Store(true)
97				return n, ErrPackUploadAborted
98			}
99		}
100		return n, err //nolint:wrapcheck // Read must preserve io.EOF for io.Reader contract
101	}
102	
103	// SetAborter registers a function that decides, on each Read, whether
104	// the upload should be cancelled mid-stream. Must be called before the
105	// observer starts receiving Reads (i.e. before being handed to
106	// PushPack); not safe to swap concurrently with active Reads.
107	func (o *packStreamObserver) SetAborter(f aborterFunc) {
108		o.aborter = f
109	}
110	
111	// Aborted reports whether the aborter triggered during this upload.
112	// Stays true after the first abort even though subsequent Reads keep
113	// returning the sentinel error; callers should check this flag to
114	// distinguish a self-imposed early stop from a server-side 4xx/5xx.
115	func (o *packStreamObserver) Aborted() bool {
116		return o.aborted.Load()
117	}
118	
119	// Close releases the observer's pipe so the Scanner goroutine drains
120	// cleanly, then closes the underlying source. Idempotent on the source

content.text: Now I understand the data flow. The bug: `consume`'s `defer pr.Close()` runs when the Scanner stops early (error), which makes the live `TeeReader` write to `pw` fail with `ErrClosedPipe`, killing the upload — despite the documented "non-fatal" contract. content.text: Critical finding: `observer.Close()` is **not** called in the bootstrap flow — so a drain-until-`pw`-closes fix would leak the goroutine. The robust fix is to make the **writer side** tolerant: once the consume goroutine closes the pipe reader (scanner error or normal completion), further tee writes must not abort the live upload. Let me create the branch and check for an existing test file.

input.command: git checkout -b fix/pack-observer-non-fatal -q && ls internal/strategy/bootstrap/pack_observer_test.go 2>&1; grep -n "func Test" internal/strategy/bootstrap/pack_observer_test.go 2>/dev/null | head

input.command:
cd /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync; go test ./internal/strategy/bootstrap/ 2>&1 | tail -3 && git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Keep a pack-observer Scanner error from aborting the upload

The observer tees the pack stream through a goroutine running packfile.Scanner.
When that Scanner stopped early (a malformed pack, or content it couldn't
parse), its deferred pr.Close() broke the pipe, so the next TeeReader write
failed with io.ErrClosedPipe and surfaced from Read — aborting the live push,
despite the documented "non-fatal for the upload" contract.

Tee through a bestEffortWriter that absorbs write failures and always reports
success, so a stopped observer can never turn into a failed upload. The bytes
keep flowing to the server and the Scanner error remains available via
ScannerError() for debugging. (This removes the spurious push failure, so
there is no longer an unclassified error for executeBatched to mishandle.)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline main..HEAD

ALTITUDE reviewer in a /simplify pass for git-sync at /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync. Check whether this fix is at the right depth. Do NOT hunt correctness bugs.

Problem: packStreamObserver tees a pack upload stream through a goroutine running packfile.Scanner. When the Scanner stopped early, its `defer pr.Close()` in consume() broke the pipe, so the next TeeReader write failed with io.ErrClosedPipe and aborted the LIVE upload — violating the documented "observation is non-fatal for the upload" contract.

Chosen fix: wrap the pipe writer in a `bestEffortWriter` that swallows write errors so the upload Read never fails from observer trouble.

Alternative considered & rejected: have consume() keep draining the pipe (io.Copy(io.Discard, pr)) instead of closing it — but observer.Close() is NOT reliably called in the bootstrap push flow (internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap.go around line 491-504 reads the observer counters and closes packReader, not the observer), so a drain-until-pw-closed approach would leak the goroutine.

Read internal/strategy/bootstrap/pack_observer.go and internal/strategy/bootstrap/bootstrap.go (~470-560, the push + observer usage). Verify: (1) is the writer-side fix the right altitude given Close() isn't reliably called? (2) is there a goroutine leak in consume() either before or after this change, and does this fix make it better/worse/neutral? Return a verdict + any findings.

<result>Now let me analyze the code structure more carefully by examining the flow and error handling:

The implementation uses a `bestEffortWriter` wrapper around the pipe writer to prevent `io.ErrClosedPipe` from bubbling up through `io.TeeReader` and terminating the upload prematurely. Let me evaluate the simplicity:

**Analysis:**

1. **Current Design (bestEffortWriter wrapper)**:
   - Lines 95-108: The `bestEffortWriter` sits between the tee and the pipe writer
   - It catches the first write error (typically `io.ErrClosedPipe` when the consume goroutine closes `pr`)
   - After the first error, it returns `(len(p), nil)` always, silently absorbing writes
   - This prevents TeeReader from seeing the error and failing the upload

2. **Why it exists** (per comments, lines 81-85):
   - Once the consume goroutine stops and closes `pr`, writes to `pw` would fail with `io.ErrClosedPipe`
   - TeeReader would surface that error on Read, aborting the live upload
   - Since observation is non-fatal, the error needs to be absorbed

3. **Could the consume() goroutine handle this differently?**
   - Line 197: `defer pr.Close()` closes the reader side
   - Line 195-197: consume runs independently, closes pr on completion
   - The pipe is unidirectional — there's no way to tell pw "close but swallow errors"

**Simplification Assessment:**

The current design is actually quite clean. Alternative approaches would be worse:

- **Alternative 1**: Don't close `pr` until the upload completes → but then consume can't signal completion, and you'd need a different synchronization mechanism
- **Alternative 2**: Use a custom Reader wrapper instead of TeeReader → same complexity, just inverted
- **Alternative 3**: Make the pipe buffered → doesn't solve the fundamental issue; once consume closes pr, pw still fails
- **Alternative 4**: Handle the error in Read() after TeeReader → impossible; TeeReader already failed and there's no recovery point

The `bestEffortWriter` is a minimal, focused solution:
- Single responsibility: absorb writes after the first failure
- No state management beyond a bool flag
- Touched only by one goroutine (the upload goroutine), no synchronization needed
- The logic is straightforward and well-commented

**Verdict: CLEAN** — No unnecessary complexity detected. The bestEffortWriter is the simplest viable solution given the concurrent architecture (separate goroutines for upload and observation, unidirectional pipe communication, and the requirement that observation failures are non-fatal to the upload).</result>

- Touched only by one goroutine (the upload goroutine), no synchronization needed
- The logic is straightforward and well-commented

**Verdict: CLEAN** — No unnecessary complexity detected. The bestEffortWriter is the simplest viable solution given the concurrent architecture (separate goroutines for upload and observation, unidirectional pipe communication, and the requirement that observation failures are non-fatal to the upload).</result>

After a thorough search of the codebase, I found **no existing reusable "swallow write errors" helper** that should replace `bestEffortWriter`. Here are my findings:

### Existing io.Writer Wrappers in `internal/`:

1. **countingWriter** (`internal/gitproto/push.go:424`) — Counts bytes; propagates write errors (not best-effort)
2. **prefixedLineWriter** (`internal/gitproto/push.go:664`) — Prepends line prefixes; propagates write errors  
3. **sessionStderr** (`internal/syncer/progress.go:358`) — Routes writes to progress reporter; propagates errors
4. **commitParentsWriter** (`internal/gitproto/commitparents.go:116`) — Buffers and hashes object data; propagates errors
5. **sshCommandError** (`internal/gitproto/ssh.go:309`) — Thread-safe error collection; propagates buffer write errors
6. **lockedBuffer** (`internal/syncer/git_http_backend_test.go:828`) — Thread-safe test buffer; propagates write errors

- **closeWriterFunc** (`internal/gitproto/ssh_test.go:203`) — Test mock that returns success unconditionally, but only used in SSH test setup

### Result:

- Propagate errors from their wrapped writer
- Only appear in test code (closeWriterFunc)

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent comes to rest with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Perfect. Now I have all the information. Let me write my verdict:

---

## ALTITUDE REVIEW VERDICT: FIX IS CORRECTLY POSITIONED

The `bestEffortWriter` wrapper solution is **the right altitude** for this problem. Here's why:

### Problem Summary (Confirmed)
- `consume()` runs in a goroutine and calls `defer pr.Close()` when it exits (line 197)
- When `consume()` encounters a Scanner error or malformed pack, it stops reading and closes the pipe reader
- The upload goroutine is still calling `io.TeeReader.Read()`, which tries to write to `pw` via the tee  
- Writing to a closed pipe fails with `io.ErrClosedPipe`, and TeeReader surfaces that as a Read error, **aborting the live upload**
- **Critical fact**: `observer.Close()` is NEVER called in bootstrap.go (lines 490-620)
  - On error path (line 503): only `packReader.Close()` is called, not observer (line 504)
  - On success path (line 613): only `packReader.Close()` is called, not observer
  - So `pw.Close()` from `Close()` (line 147) never happens in practice

### Why the Writer-Side Fix is Right
1. **Matches the actual control flow**: Since `observer.Close()` isn't reliably called, the consume goroutine's `defer pr.Close()` is the only thing that closes the pipe. A drain-until-pw-closed approach (the rejected alternative) would rely on Close() being called, which doesn't happen. The writer-side fix works regardless.

2. **Non-fatal semantic enforcement**: The code already documents "observation is non-fatal for the upload" (lines 82-85). Wrapping the writer enforces this at the point where the contract is breached, preventing observer trouble from escaping to the caller.

3. **Minimal scope**: The fix is confined to the TeeReader write side—doesn't touch consume(), doesn't add goroutine management complexity, doesn't change the observer's public interface or Close() semantics.

### Goroutine Leak Analysis

**Before the fix:**
- `consume()` can leak if it closes `pr` and TeeReader subsequently fails on write, aborting the upload
- The goroutine exits normally (defer close(o.done) runs), but the runtime has already hit the io.ErrClosedPipe error from TeeReader
- **Result**: Goroutine eventually exits, but in a failure cascade that crashes the upload—a "leak-by-abort"

**After the fix:**
- `bestEffortWriter` swallows the close-pipe error after the first one (line 104-107)
- TeeReader never sees the error, so upload continues uninterrupted
- `consume()` still exits normally (defer close(o.done) line 196)
- **Result**: Goroutine exits cleanly after Scanner stops, o.done is closed, counters are readable

**Verdict on leaks**: No structural goroutine leak exists in either case—the issue is the **abort-by-error**, not a hanging goroutine. The fix makes this neutral-to-better: it prevents the error cascade that would have masked a clean exit.

### Summary
✅ **Fix depth is correct**: Altitude is the writer wrapper, not observer lifecycle management, because Close() is unreliable in this codebase.  
✅ **No new leaks introduced**: bestEffortWriter doesn't add goroutine management; it just prevents observer errors from escaping.  
✅ **Matches actual usage**: Respects the "observation is non-fatal" contract that the bootstrap flow assumes but doesn't enforce.

No architectural changes needed. The fix is fit for purpose.</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>21607</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>17</tool_uses><duration_ms>36662</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

content.text: All three M4 reviewers clean — altitude confirms the writer-side fix is correct precisely because `Close()` isn't reliably called, and introduces no leak. No `/simplify` edits. Pushing and opening the M4 PR:

input.command:
cd /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync; git push -u origin fix/pack-observer-non-fatal 2>&1 | tail -2 && gh pr create --base main --head fix/pack-observer-non-fatal --title "Keep a pack-observer Scanner error from aborting the upload" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

`packStreamObserver` tees the pack upload stream through a goroutine running `packfile.Scanner`. When that Scanner stopped early — a malformed pack, or content it couldn't parse — `consume`'s `defer pr.Close()` broke the pipe. The next `io.TeeReader` write then failed with `io.ErrClosedPipe`, surfaced from `Read`, and **aborted the live push** — directly contradicting the documented *"non-fatal for the upload"* contract. The resulting spurious error also wasn't classified as subdividable by `executeBatched`.

## Fix

Tee through a small `bestEffortWriter` that absorbs write failures and always reports success, so a stopped observer can never turn into a failed upload. The bytes keep flowing to the server; the Scanner error stays available via `ScannerError()` for debugging.

### Why the writer side, not the pipe lifecycle

`observer.Close()` is **not** reliably called in the bootstrap push flow (the error and success paths close the underlying `packReader`, not the observer), so the obvious alternative — keep draining the pipe with `io.Copy(io.Discard, pr)` until `pw` closes — would block forever and leak the goroutine. Absorbing failures at the writer boundary works regardless of whether `Close()` runs, and removes the spurious push failure entirely (so there's no longer an unclassified error for `executeBatched` to mishandle).

## Tests

`TestPackStreamObserverScannerErrorDoesNotAbortUpload` feeds non-pack bytes (Scanner fails parsing the header mid-stream) and asserts every byte still comes back out of `Read` with no error, while `ScannerError()` records the failure.

content.subject: H1: Credential token leak in usage output
content.description: Token flags use env value as pflag default, leaking secrets into --help and CI usage dumps. flags.go:28-39, convert_sha256.go:77-82. Verify against main, fix on branch, codex review, simplify, PR.

content.subject: H2: SSH argument injection via destination
content.description: ssh argv lacks -- separator and destination/port validation; ssh://-oProxyCommand=evil URL → RCE. ssh.go:136-162. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: H3: FF ancestry check walks have-pruned store
content.description: ReachesCommit not pruning-aware; FF misclassified as ActionBlock or hard-fails. planner.go:408-450 via syncer.go:816. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: H4: Nil-deref in checkpoint error path
content.description: Error message dereferences commit after it's nil. checkpoint.go:52-55. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M1: --source-url + positional target rejected
content.description: Positionals consumed by fixed index; fix pattern exists in convert_sha256.go:115-134. syncplan.go:51-56, bootstrap.go:33-38, probe.go:32-37 (silent). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M2: git-credential host omits port
content.description: Uses ep.Hostname() instead of ep.Host. auth.go:180. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M3: unstable.FetchRequest drops Scope.Mappings
content.description: buildFetchConfig never sets Mappings. unstable/client.go:319-337. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M4: pack-observer error aborts healthy upload
content.description: Scanner error → deferred pr.Close() fails TeeReader body read, killing push; not treated as subdividable. pack_observer.go:171-197. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M5: consumeV2FetchPack treats premature EOF as success
content.description: Response with no packfile returns nil. fetch.go:323-376; sibling openV2PackStream handles correctly. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M6: internalbridge exported, bypasses validation
content.description: Stable types alias into it; exposes Run/Probe skipping Validate(). types.go:156-186, internalbridge/config.go. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L1: connection/goroutine leaks on error paths
content.description: newSession leaks conns on partial setup failure (syncer.go:677-727); MeasureMemory ticker goroutine leaks (measurement.go:36-56). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L2: BuildPlans mutates caller's managed map
content.description: planner.go:136 mutates vs :203 defensively copies. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L3: token-refresh doc/context/proxy issues
content.description: getTokenWithRefresh doc comment opposite of behavior (entiredb.go:112-114); refresh ignores caller context and ProxyFromEnvironment (entiredb.go:93,180-185). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L4: git-sync-bench exit code and -1 sentinels
content.description: Exits 0 when all runs failed; emits -1 sentinels into JSON. cmd/git-sync-bench/main.go:204-292. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L5: tokenstore uses syscall.Flock (no Windows)
content.description: internal/auth/tokenstore.go uses syscall.Flock; public library won't compile on Windows. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L6: drainTrailingNAKs/auth-probe error handling
content.description: drainTrailingNAKs discards Peek transport errors (fetch.go:583-594); receive-pack auth probe only reacts to 401 not 403 (smarthttp.go:593-673). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L7: go.mod and test hygiene
content.description: go.mod pins go 1.26.2 vs go 1.26+toolchain; ExampleClient_Sync real network I/O; client_test.go tb.Fatalf from goroutines; Sync/PlanRequest.Validate duplicates. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

attachment.snippet:
1	package bootstrap
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"io"
7		"sync"
8		"sync/atomic"
9	
10		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/format/packfile"
11	)
12	
13	// ErrPackUploadAborted is returned from packStreamObserver.Read when
14	// the configured aborter says the upload is projected to exceed the
15	// target body limit. Surfaces up through the HTTP transport as a
16	// generic body-read error; bootstrap's push-failed branch checks the
17	// observer's Aborted() flag to distinguish "we cut it" from a
18	// server-side 413 / 500 / network failure.
19	var ErrPackUploadAborted = errors.New("pack upload aborted early: projected to exceed target body limit")
20	
21	// packStreamObserver wraps the pack stream handed to PushPack with two
22	// instruments:
23	//
24	//  1. a byte counter (replacing the simpler packReadCounter), so the
25	//     bootstrap loop still learns how much data went up before a 413
26	//     for post-rejection sizing;
27	//  2. a streaming pack-format observer that tees the bytes through
28	//     packfile.Scanner running on a goroutine, exposing how many of
29	//     the pack header's advertised objects have actually been sent
30	//     across the wire so far.
31	//
32	// The (objects-sent, bytes-sent, total-objects) triple lets later
33	// callers project the real size before the server cuts the upload —
34	// which is the prerequisite for a mid-stream early abort. Until that
35	// abort path is added, the observer is a strict superset of the
36	// previous packReadCounter and only reports observed counters.
37	//
38	// The Scanner runs on a goroutine reading from the tee'd pipe; if it
39	// falls behind the HTTP upload, the unbuffered pipe back-pressures the
40	// upload until Scanner catches up. zlib decoding inside Scanner is the
41	// dominant cost and runs at 200–500 MB/s per core on modern hardware,
42	// which leaves substantial headroom over typical wire upload speeds.
43	// If profiling later shows the observer bottlenecking real pushes, the
44	// fix is a custom format walker that skips Scanner's per-object SHA-1
45	// hashing — but the zlib walk itself is unavoidable because pack
46	// objects don't record their compressed size.
47	type packStreamObserver struct {
48		src io.ReadCloser
49		tee io.Reader
50		pw  *io.PipeWriter
51	
52		bytes        atomic.Int64
53		objectsSent  atomic.Int64
54		totalObjects atomic.Int64
55	
56		headerOnce  sync.Once
57		headerReady chan struct{}
58		done        chan struct{}
59		scannerErr  atomic.Pointer[error]
60	
61		aborter aborterFunc
62		aborted atomic.Bool
63	}
64	
65	// aborterFunc is consulted on every Read to decide whether the upload
66	// should be cancelled mid-stream. Receives the latest counters so it
67	// can project the final pack size from the bytes-per-object ratio so
68	// far. Return true to abort the upload; the observer surfaces
69	// ErrPackUploadAborted from its next Read and refuses to give up
70	// further bytes.
71	type aborterFunc func(bytesSent, objectsSent, totalObjects int64) bool
72	
73	func newPackStreamObserver(src io.ReadCloser) *packStreamObserver {
74		pr, pw := io.Pipe()
75		obs := &packStreamObserver{
76			src:         src,
77			pw:          pw,
78			headerReady: make(chan struct{}),
79			done:        make(chan struct{}),
80		}
81		obs.tee = io.TeeReader(src, pw)
82		go obs.consume(pr)
83		return obs
84	}
85	
86	func (o *packStreamObserver) Read(p []byte) (int, error) {
87		if o.aborted.Load() {
88			return 0, ErrPackUploadAborted
89		}
90		n, err := o.tee.Read(p)
91		if n > 0 {
92			o.bytes.Add(int64(n))
93		}
94		if err == nil && o.aborter != nil {
95			if o.aborter(o.bytes.Load(), o.objectsSent.Load(), o.totalObjects.Load()) {
96				o.aborted.Store(true)
97				return n, ErrPackUploadAborted
98			}
99		}
100		return n, err //nolint:wrapcheck // Read must preserve io.EOF for io.Reader contract
101	}
102	
103	// SetAborter registers a function that decides, on each Read, whether
104	// the upload should be cancelled mid-stream. Must be called before the
105	// observer starts receiving Reads (i.e. before being handed to
106	// PushPack); not safe to swap concurrently with active Reads.
107	func (o *packStreamObserver) SetAborter(f aborterFunc) {
108		o.aborter = f
109	}
110	
111	// Aborted reports whether the aborter triggered during this upload.
112	// Stays true after the first abort even though subsequent Reads keep
113	// returning the sentinel error; callers should check this flag to
114	// distinguish a self-imposed early stop from a server-side 4xx/5xx.
115	func (o *packStreamObserver) Aborted() bool {
116		return o.aborted.Load()
117	}
118	
119	// Close releases the observer's pipe so the Scanner goroutine drains
120	// cleanly, then closes the underlying source. Idempotent on the source
121	// side (the wrapped ReadCloser may itself be a closeOnce wrapper).
122	func (o *packStreamObserver) Close() error {
123		_ = o.pw.Close()
124		<-o.done
125		if err := o.src.Close(); err != nil {
126			return fmt.Errorf("close pack source: %w", err)
127		}
128		return nil
129	}
130	
131	// HeaderReady returns a channel that closes once the pack header has
132	// been parsed and TotalObjects is populated. Callers that need the
133	// total before bytes start flowing can wait on this; callers that just
134	// poll TotalObjects opportunistically can ignore it.
135	func (o *packStreamObserver) HeaderReady() <-chan struct{} {
136		return o.headerReady
137	}
138	
139	// Bytes returns the cumulative bytes pulled from the source so far.
140	func (o *packStreamObserver) Bytes() int64 {
141		return o.bytes.Load()
142	}
143	
144	// ObjectsSent returns the number of objects fully observed by the
145	// Scanner. Scanner emits one ObjectSection per object after fully
146	// walking that object's zlib stream, so this is "objects whose bytes
147	// have all been read by us" — not "objects whose bytes are confirmed
148	// landed on the server". The two are within one zlib block of each
149	// other in practice and the distinction does not matter for sizing.
150	func (o *packStreamObserver) ObjectsSent() int64 {
151		return o.objectsSent.Load()
152	}
153	
154	// TotalObjects returns the object count from the pack header, or 0 if
155	// the header has not been parsed yet (or was malformed).
156	func (o *packStreamObserver) TotalObjects() int64 {
157		return o.totalObjects.Load()
158	}
159	
160	// ScannerError returns the first error the Scanner produced, if any.
161	// A non-nil error means observation stopped early — counters above
162	// will not advance further. Useful for debugging; non-fatal for the
163	// upload itself, which is driven by Read on the tee.
164	func (o *packStreamObserver) ScannerError() error {
165		if p := o.scannerErr.Load(); p != nil {
166			return *p
167		}
168		return nil
169	}
170	
171	func (o *packStreamObserver) consume(pr *io.PipeReader) {
172		defer close(o.done)
173		defer pr.Close()
174	
175		s := packfile.NewScanner(pr)
176		for s.Scan() {
177			d := s.Data()
178			switch d.Section {
179			case packfile.HeaderSection:
180				if h, ok := d.Value().(packfile.Header); ok {
181					o.totalObjects.Store(int64(h.ObjectsQty))
182					o.headerOnce.Do(func() { close(o.headerReady) })
183				}
184			case packfile.ObjectSection:
185				o.objectsSent.Add(1)
186			case packfile.FooterSection:
187				// Footer (pack checksum) carries no per-object signal; the
188				// final ObjectSection has already incremented objectsSent.
189			}
190		}
191		if err := s.Error(); err != nil && !errors.Is(err, io.EOF) && !errors.Is(err, io.ErrClosedPipe) {
192			o.scannerErr.Store(&err)
193		}
194		// Ensure HeaderReady is closed even on a malformed pack so callers
195		// waiting on it don't block forever.
196		o.headerOnce.Do(func() { close(o.headerReady) })
197	}

content.text: Let me read the sibling `openV2PackStream` to see the correct premature-EOF handling:

The asymmetry is clear: on premature flush/EOF without a packfile, `openV2PackStream` returns `io.ErrUnexpectedEOF` but `consumeV2FetchPack` returns `nil` (success). But the consume version's doc says *"Returns nil for empty / no-pack responses"* — so I must verify this isn't a deliberate up-to-date path before turning it into an error. content.text: Both `fetchToStoreV2` and `fetchPackV2` already return `git.NoErrAlreadyUpToDate` *early* when `len(wants)==0` — so once a fetch command is actually sent, a flush/EOF without a packfile is anomalous in both. The fix (match the sibling's `io.ErrUnexpectedEOF`) is sound. Let me confirm no test expects the old nil-on-no-pack success:

input.command: cd /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync; grep -n "consumeV2FetchPack\|storeV2FetchPack\|func Test.*ConsumeV2\|func Test.*StoreV2\|func Test.*FetchToStoreV2" internal/gitproto/fetch_test.go

content.text: These existing tests cover the `expectPackfile` paths (ack-without-ready, ready-without-packfile) — both already error. The gap the review flags is the **bare flush / EOF with no packfile section** (`expectPackfile==false`), which returns nil. Since `wants>0` is guaranteed by the time we reach here, that's always anomalous. Creating the branch and applying the fix:

content.text: Codex clean — confirms it aligns the store path with the stream path. For a 2-line behavior fix that makes the two siblings consistent, `/simplify` is self-evident: the change *reduces* divergence between `consumeV2FetchPack` and `openV2PackStream`. (Their broader envelope-parsing duplication is real but consolidating it is a separate refactor, out of scope here.) No edits. Pushing and opening the M5 PR:

input.command:
cd /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync; git push -u origin fix/v2-fetch-premature-eof 2>&1 | tail -2 && gh pr create --base main --head fix/v2-fetch-premature-eof --title "Treat a pack-less v2 fetch response as an error, not success" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

`consumeV2FetchPack` returned `nil` when the protocol-v2 fetch response ended — a bare flush or EOF — without ever delivering a `packfile` section. So a truncated or pack-less response let a fetch report **success having stored nothing**. Its streaming sibling `openV2PackStream` correctly treats the same situation as `io.ErrUnexpectedEOF`.

By the time control reaches either function, `wants` is non-empty (`fetchToStoreV2`/`fetchPackV2` both return `git.NoErrAlreadyUpToDate` early when there's nothing to fetch), so the server genuinely owes us a packfile section — a response without one is anomalous.

## Fix

Return `io.ErrUnexpectedEOF` on the no-packfile flush/EOF paths in `consumeV2FetchPack`, matching `openV2PackStream`. The `expectPackfile` (post-`ready`) branch keeps its existing, more specific message, and an empty-but-present pack still flows through the normal `packfile` path unchanged.

## Tests

content.text: `internalbridge` is **not** under `internal/` — so despite its name it's a fully importable public package, and the stable types alias *into* it (`type RefKind = internalbridge.RefKind`), exposing `Run`/`Probe`/`Config` so external callers bypass `Validate()`. The focused fix that addresses the root cause: move it under `internal/` so it's genuinely unimportable externally (the root-package aliases keep working). Let me check the blast radius.

content.subject: H2: SSH argument injection via destination
content.description: ssh argv lacks -- separator and destination/port validation; ssh://-oProxyCommand=evil URL → RCE. ssh.go:136-162. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: H3: FF ancestry check walks have-pruned store
content.description: ReachesCommit not pruning-aware; FF misclassified as ActionBlock or hard-fails. planner.go:408-450 via syncer.go:816. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: H4: Nil-deref in checkpoint error path
content.description: Error message dereferences commit after it's nil. checkpoint.go:52-55. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M1: --source-url + positional target rejected
content.description: Positionals consumed by fixed index; fix pattern exists in convert_sha256.go:115-134. syncplan.go:51-56, bootstrap.go:33-38, probe.go:32-37 (silent). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M2: git-credential host omits port
content.description: Uses ep.Hostname() instead of ep.Host. auth.go:180. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M3: unstable.FetchRequest drops Scope.Mappings
content.description: buildFetchConfig never sets Mappings. unstable/client.go:319-337. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M4: pack-observer error aborts healthy upload
content.description: Scanner error → deferred pr.Close() fails TeeReader body read, killing push; not treated as subdividable. pack_observer.go:171-197. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M5: consumeV2FetchPack treats premature EOF as success
content.description: Response with no packfile returns nil. fetch.go:323-376; sibling openV2PackStream handles correctly. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: M6: internalbridge exported, bypasses validation
content.description: Stable types alias into it; exposes Run/Probe skipping Validate(). types.go:156-186, internalbridge/config.go. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L1: connection/goroutine leaks on error paths
content.description: newSession leaks conns on partial setup failure (syncer.go:677-727); MeasureMemory ticker goroutine leaks (measurement.go:36-56). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L2: BuildPlans mutates caller's managed map
content.description: planner.go:136 mutates vs :203 defensively copies. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L3: token-refresh doc/context/proxy issues
content.description: getTokenWithRefresh doc comment opposite of behavior (entiredb.go:112-114); refresh ignores caller context and ProxyFromEnvironment (entiredb.go:93,180-185). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L4: git-sync-bench exit code and -1 sentinels
content.description: Exits 0 when all runs failed; emits -1 sentinels into JSON. cmd/git-sync-bench/main.go:204-292. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L5: tokenstore uses syscall.Flock (no Windows)
content.description: internal/auth/tokenstore.go uses syscall.Flock; public library won't compile on Windows. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L6: drainTrailingNAKs/auth-probe error handling
content.description: drainTrailingNAKs discards Peek transport errors (fetch.go:583-594); receive-pack auth probe only reacts to 401 not 403 (smarthttp.go:593-673). Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.subject: L7: go.mod and test hygiene
content.description: go.mod pins go 1.26.2 vs go 1.26+toolchain; ExampleClient_Sync real network I/O; client_test.go tb.Fatalf from goroutines; Sync/PlanRequest.Validate duplicates. Verify, fix, codex, simplify, PR.

content.text: Codex: "pure package relocation, full suite passes." For a pure relocation there's nothing for `/simplify` to change — and it actually *resolves* the altitude concern: `internalbridge` can now evolve freely since the root-package aliases are its only (controlled) public contact. Pushing and opening the M6 PR:

input.command:
cd /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync; git push -u origin fix/internalbridge-truly-internal 2>&1 | tail -2 && gh pr create --base main --head fix/internalbridge-truly-internal --title "Move internalbridge under internal/ so it is truly unimportable" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

`internalbridge` lived at the **module root**, so despite its name it was a public, importable package. An external caller could:

- construct an `internalbridge.Config` via `SyncConfig`/`ProbeConfig` and call `internalbridge.Run`/`Probe` directly, **skipping the stable API's `Validate()` entirely**; and
- the stable types are aliases into it (`type RefKind = internalbridge.RefKind`, …), which froze it into the v1 public surface.

## Fix

Move it to `internal/internalbridge`. Go's internal-package rule now bars any out-of-module importer (compiler-enforced), closing the `Validate()` bypass. The root-package type aliases keep resolving for external users — an alias to an internal type stays usable *through the alias* — so `gitsync.RefKind` etc. are unaffected, while `internalbridge` is free to evolve behind them.

The package name is unchanged, so only the three in-module import paths move (`client.go`, `types.go`, `unstable/client.go`); no call sites or behavior change.

## Verification

`go build ./...`, `go vet ./...`, and the full `go test ./...` all pass. The internal boundary is compiler-enforced, so an out-of-module import of the bridge would now fail to compile.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2

input.command:
cd /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync; git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Release session resources on partial-setup and error paths

Two leaks in the syncer session lifecycle:

  - newSession opened the source transport (and started the memory-measurement
    ticker goroutine) before listing refs and setting up the target. If any of
    those steps failed it returned nil, err, so the caller had no session to
    finish() — leaking the source conn (an SSH transport spawns a process), a
    half-open target conn, and the ticker goroutine.
  - finish() never stopped the measurement ticker; it was only stopped when a
    Result was built, so any early error return from runSync et al. leaked the
    goroutine even on a fully-constructed session.

Add a success-guarded deferred cleanup in newSession that calls finish() unless
the session is handed back, hand the target conn to the session as soon as it
is opened so cleanup can close it, and have finish() also invoke the
sync.Once-guarded measurementDone so the ticker stops on every path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
codex exec review --base main 2>&1 | tail -14

toolUseResult.prompt:
SIMPLIFICATION reviewer in a /simplify pass for git-sync at /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync. Review ONLY this change for unnecessary complexity. Do NOT hunt correctness bugs.

Change in internal/syncer/syncer.go: newSession() now builds s.target in two steps — `s.target = &targetSession{conn: targetConn}` right after opening the conn, then assigns s.target.adv/refMap/features/policy/pusher individually after the ref-listing steps (previously a single struct literal at the end). Also added a `success bool` guard with `defer { if !success { s.finish() } }`. Read internal/syncer/syncer.go around newSession (627-760) and finish (~592). Assess whether the two-step target construction or the success guard is more complex than necessary. Return findings (file, line, summary, simpler form) or say clean.

input.prompt:
ALTITUDE reviewer in a /simplify pass for git-sync at /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync. Check whether this fix is at the right depth. Do NOT hunt correctness bugs.

Problem: newSession() opened the source transport and started a memory-measurement ticker goroutine, then could return nil,err on later setup failures — leaking conns (SSH spawns a process) and the goroutine, because the caller had no session to finish(). Also finish() never stopped the measurement ticker, so early error returns from runSync leaked the goroutine even on a built session.

Fix: success-guarded `defer s.finish()` inside newSession; finish() now also calls the sync.Once-guarded measurementDone(); target conn handed to the session as soon as opened.

Read internal/syncer/syncer.go (newSession 627-760, finish ~592, the Run/Probe/Bootstrap callers ~760-1090 that already `defer s.finish()`). Questions: (1) Is consolidating all teardown into finish() (including the measurement goroutine) the right altitude? (2) Is the success-guard defer the idiomatic depth, or is there a cleaner structural fix? (3) Does finish() now being called both by newSession's defer (on failure) and the caller's defer (on success) create any double-finish concern worth noting? Return a verdict + findings.

<result>Perfect. Now I have a clear picture. Let me assess this change:

## Simplification Review: git-sync internal/syncer/syncer.go

**File:** /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/syncer.go

### Analysis

The change splits target construction into two steps and adds a success guard. Here's my assessment:

**Two-Step Target Construction (lines 721-741):**
- **Current:** `s.target = &targetSession{conn: targetConn}` (line 721), then individual field assignments (lines 734-741)
- **Justification:** The comment at lines 719-720 explicitly explains the rationale: "Hand the conn to the session immediately so the deferred cleanup closes it even if a ref-listing step below fails." This is a *deliberate design choice* to ensure the connection gets closed via `finish()` in the defer cleanup, even if errors occur in the subsequent ref-listing steps (lines 724-731).
- **Assessment:** This is **not unnecessary complexity**—it's essential for resource safety. Moving to a single struct literal would mean the connection wouldn't be owned by the session until the very end, risking leaks if earlier steps fail.

**Success Guard (lines 670-675, 771):**
- **Current:** `success := false` at line 670, defer cleanup at 671-675, set `success = true` at 771
- **Alternative:** Could be written as `defer func() { if err == nil { ... } }()` with return statements, but that requires threading `err` through—less clean.
- **Assessment:** The `success` guard is straightforward and idiomatic. It's cleaner than checking `err` in defer since there are multiple error return paths. No simplification needed.

### Verdict

**CLEAN** — No unnecessary complexity found. Both patterns serve explicit purposes:
1. The two-step construction ensures connection cleanup even on mid-setup failures (resource safety)
2. The success guard cleanly encapsulates the defer-based cleanup pattern without threading error state

The code is well-commented and the patterns are justified.</result>

<result>Perfect. Now I have the full context. Let me provide my findings:

## Simplify Review: Reuse Analysis

**CLEAN** — The change reuses existing patterns correctly:

1. **finish() as centralized cleanup point**: This is the right pattern. All four public entry points (Run, Bootstrap, Probe, Fetch) already use `defer s.finish()` (lines 783, 1067, 1093, 1107). The `finish()` method (line 595-608) is the single place that releases:
   - `s.progress.terminate()` for the live ticker goroutine
   - `s.measurementDone()` for the memory-measurement ticker
   - `s.sourceConn.Close()` and `s.target.conn.Close()` for transports

2. **success-guarded defer cleanup pattern**: The `success := false` + `defer { if !success { s.finish() } }` pattern (lines 670-675) is appropriately scoped. It's already documented in context (lines 666-669): "If we return an error partway through setup the caller has no session to finish(), so release everything here unless we hand the session back." This prevents double-cleanup since callers only invoke `defer s.finish()` after successful return.

3. **Target connection handed early**: Handing `targetConn` to `s.target` immediately (line 721) is correct — the comment (lines 719-720) explains it ensures deferred cleanup closes it even if ref-listing fails.

No scattered Close calls exist outside `finish()` — all resource release is consolidated. No additional helper or pattern is needed.</result>
